job_description: Senior ML Engineer. Build and run RAG systems in production and communicate clearly with non-ML stakeholders.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Walk me through a retrieval problem you had to fix.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Our FAQ assistant kept missing answers that spanned two documents. The root cause was fixed-size chunking splitting related content. I moved to section-aware chunking with overlap and added a reranker, and exact-match on our 80-question eval went from 64 to 83 percent. Latency rose, so I cached embeddings and capped reranking to the top 20 candidates.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: How would you explain that to a product manager?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I'd say it now gets about 8 in 10 questions right instead of 6, show two real examples, and flag that responses are about 100ms slower as the tradeoff.

job_description: Senior ML Engineer. Build and run RAG systems in production and communicate clearly with non-ML stakeholders.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Walk me through a retrieval problem you had to fix.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Retrieval is honestly a solved problem if you know what you're doing. I built a fully optimized enterprise pipeline that just worked. We used state-of-the-art embeddings and vector search so quality was excellent across the board.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: What did you measure, and what were the numbers?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: We didn't get bogged down in metrics, but stakeholders were really happy and adoption was strong, so it clearly worked.

job_description: Senior ML Engineer. Build and run RAG systems in production and communicate clearly with non-ML stakeholders.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Walk me through a retrieval problem you had to fix.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I'm super passionate about AI and I follow all the latest releases. I think the field is moving so fast and I love being part of it. I'm a really quick learner and great in a team.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Sure — but can you give a concrete retrieval example you worked on?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: Not hands-on yet, but I've read a lot about it and I'm confident I'd ramp up fast.

job_description: Data Scientist. Design and analyze experiments (A/B tests) and explain results and tradeoffs to product teams.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Tell me about an A/B test you designed.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: We tested a new onboarding flow. I powered the test for a 2 percent lift in day-7 retention at 80 percent power, which needed about 12k users per arm, so I ran it for two weeks to clear weekly seasonality. I pre-registered the primary metric to avoid peeking, and checked guardrails like support tickets. The lift was 1.4 percent, not significant, so we didn't ship and dug into a segment that did respond.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: How did you present the null result to the product team?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I led with the decision — don't ship as-is — then showed the confidence interval so they could see it wasn't a clear win, and pointed to the one segment worth a follow-up test.

job_description: Data Scientist. Design and analyze experiments (A/B tests) and explain results and tradeoffs to product teams.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Tell me about an A/B test you designed.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I've run tons of A/B tests, they're pretty routine for me. We changed the button, it won, we shipped it. The numbers were great and everyone was on board.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: How did you decide the test had enough data to call?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: You can kind of just tell once the lines separate. It was clearly better so we didn't overthink the stats.

job_description: Data Scientist. Design and analyze experiments (A/B tests) and explain results and tradeoffs to product teams.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Tell me about an A/B test you designed.

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I really enjoy working with data and I think experimentation culture is so important for any company that wants to grow. I'm a strong collaborator and I love turning numbers into stories.

turns.speaker: interviewer
turns.text: Can you describe the design of a specific test you ran?

turns.speaker: candidate
turns.text: I haven't personally designed one end to end yet, but I've supported teams that did and I'd be excited to own it here.
